chemicals.name: Based on the provided sources, alcohol contains specific chemical compounds that are highly dangerous to cats

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Cats are exceptionally sensitive to ethanol. Even a tiny amount—as little as a single tablespoon or teaspoon—is enough to put a cat into a coma or cause severe, irreversible liver and brain damage35. The higher the proof (concentration) of the alcohol, the more severe the toxic effects will be
chemicals.description: in cats: Ethanol is a small, polar molecule that is quickly absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract and easily crosses the blood-brain barrier34. Cats lack robust activity of the enzyme alcohol dehydrogenase, meaning their bodies cannot efficiently metabolize and clear the ethanol4. This inability to process the chemical leads to profound central nervous system (CNS) depression34. Additionally
chemicals.name: Ethanol (Ethyl Alcohol)

chemicals.concentration_notes: or potency: Just like ethanol, even a tiny amount of exposure to methanol or isopropanol is extremely toxic to cats and can easily be fatal
chemicals.description: in cats: Similar to ethanol, these compounds are rapidly absorbed into the bloodstream and act as powerful central nervous system depressants, overwhelming the cat's limited metabolic detoxification pathways and causing systemic cellular toxicity36. chemicals.name: Methanol and Isopropanol

description: Alcohol (ethanol) is highly toxic to cats, far more so than to humans, because cats lack robust alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme activity in the liver — meaning they cannot metabolise ethanol efficiently. Even tiny amounts are dangerous. Ethanol is absorbed rapidly through the gastrointestinal tract, crosses the blood-brain barrier acting as a severe CNS depressant, causes acute liver injury, triggers metabolic acidosis, and leads to dangerous heat loss through vasodilation. Symptoms typically appear within 30 to 60 minutes of ingestion. Beyond alcoholic beverages, hidden sources include raw yeast dough (which ferments in the stomach), rotting fruit, and certain mouthwashes or hand sanitisers (which may contain methanol or isopropanol, both even more toxic than ethanol).
name: Alcohol
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Disorientation, loss of coordination (ataxia), lethargy, muscle tremors, vomiting, diarrhea, abnormally low body temperature (hypothermia), and metabolic acidosis; severe cases progress to respiratory depression, seizures, coma, and death.
Tiny amounts matter: As little as one teaspoon of alcohol can put a cat into a coma and cause severe liver or brain damage.
Hidden sources: Raw bread/pizza dough, rotting fruit, mouthwash, and some cleaning products — not just alcoholic drinks.

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Upset
symptoms.notes: Alcohol directly irritates the gastrointestinal tract34. If the source of the alcohol is raw yeast dough, the cat may also suffer from severe gastric distension (bloating) and abdominal pain as the fermenting yeast produces carbon dioxide gas in the warm environment of the stomach

symptoms.name: Neurological Impairment
symptoms.notes: Ethanol is rapidly absorbed into the bloodstream and easily crosses the blood-brain barrier1. Because cats lack robust alcohol dehydrogenase enzyme activity to metabolize the chemical efficiently, this leads to profound CNS depression, resulting in "drunk-like" stumbling, extreme tiredness, tremors,

symptoms.name: Hypothermia
symptoms.notes: Alcohol induces peripheral vasodilation, which widens the blood vessels near the surface of the skin1. This causes the cat's body to rapidly lose heat, dropping its core temperature to dangerous levels

symptoms.name: Metabolic Acidosis
symptoms.notes: The liver's attempt to process the ethanol produces acetic acid1. This, combined with an accumulation of lactic acid due to poor systemic perfusion, dangerously lowers the pH of the cat's blood
symptoms.onset: Within 1 hour (progressing as the alcohol is metabolized)

symptoms.name: Respiratory Depression
symptoms.notes: As CNS depression worsens, the cat's normal respiratory drive is suppressed1. This leads to shallow, labored breathing that can culminate in complete respiratory failure and death

symptoms.name: Acute Organ Damage
symptoms.notes: Because a cat's liver cannot efficiently clear alcohol, just a tiny amount—as little as a single teaspoon or tablespoon—can quickly overwhelm the liver and brain, leading to irreversible organ failure and death

treatments.description: of the procedure: Severe ethanol intoxication depresses the central nervous system, which can lead to poor breathing, respiratory depression, and respiratory failure12. The veterinary team will provide mechanical ventilation (artificial respiration) if the cat is struggling to breathe, and administer medications to address any life-threatening cardiac arrhythmias23. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Emergency Stabilization (Respiratory and Cardiovascular Support)
treatments.notes: This is the absolute highest priority. Patients with clinical signs of ethanol toxicosis must be stabilized, and life-threatening conditions must be corrected before any attempts are made to decontaminate the stomach

treatments.description: of the procedure: The cat is placed on continuous IV fluids to aggressively correct electrolyte imbalances and the abnormal blood acidity (metabolic acidosis) caused by the alcohol1.... Dextrose (sugar) is often supplemented in the fluids to correct severe hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), and the fluid therapy promotes diuresis (increased urine output) to hasten the elimination of ethanol from the
treatments.name: Metabolic Correction and Excretion Support
treatments.notes: Death from alcohol or raw dough toxicosis is typically due to the systemic metabolic effects of the ethanol, making this metabolic correction crucial to survival

treatments.description: of the procedure: Alcohol induces peripheral vasodilation, leading to rapid heat loss and dangerous hypothermia1. The veterinary team will use active warming measures to restore and maintain the cat's normal core body temperature3. Any situation-specific
treatments.name: Thermoregulation
treatments.notes: Careful monitoring for hypothermia is especially critical if cold water treatments (see below) are used for raw dough ingestion, as the cold water can further drop the cat's body temperature

treatments.description: of the procedure: If the alcohol or raw yeast dough was ingested very recently (e.g., within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is completely asymptomatic, the veterinarian may induce vomiting to expel the substance67. If raw bread dough is the culprit and vomiting is unsuccessful due to its sticky, glutinous nature, the veterinarian may flush the stomach with cold water to temporarily slow the yeast's fer
treatments.name: Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination (If Applicable)
treatments.notes: Hydrogen peroxide is strictly contraindicated for inducing vomiting in cats because it can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastritis; veterinarians will typically use safe α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonists like dexmedetomidine instead7.
